author: @scottdaniels5165
text: I don't agree with the idea of human power for accomplishing tasks. Prosperity is directly related to energy available. Primitive societies are limited to human power, and more advanced ones make use of animal and some water power. The very limited amount of energy that can be supplied by a person can be far more cheaply achieved with small solar panel in most climates. Human power should be reserved for tasks that cannot be achieved any other way, e.g. what robots or machines cannot do.

author: @kadmow
text:
- a home gravity battery - makes it possible to "chop wood or make a smoothie" - at a later date, enjoy all that hard work...   
(only do as a fun project, don't expect any real ROI.

Realising that to do a "real amount of work" - gravity really is a poor store at "household scales" - even lifting the whole house 2 metres may only store... (house weight depending - lets assume 100tonnes) ....a fraction of a kWh (0.273 kWh per metre)- a 50-60kg battery stores  10kWh, my home solar system is currently exporting 20*30kWh per day (cloud and weather depending), now that is a lot of weights lifted a lot of metres....  (it seems that house  lifters charge too much for "not a lot of work,   500watts, for 33 minutes will also lit a house 1 metre, a few gym sessions may be all the industry needs,llol..) 

(I have "always thought" a power storing-  endless climbing wall would be a great gym tool - as the climber passes "some point on the wall, the wall starts passively - rolling around - lifting a mass in the background makes some sense of an otherwise senseless activity (an air or hydraulic governor can provide increased non-conservative resistance if needed..)....   At skyscraper scales, "human powered lifts, or resetting the "free counterweights" -  not requiring the sort of infrastructure (or energy levels) of "grid scale projects" where pumped hydro is probably more  realisable - yeah nah, not happening. 

Everything needed lives inside a cuckoo clock - just make it all more heavy duty - Rob has included so many accessories, regulators or power conversion methods in many previous videos.  )

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text: Very based reasoning. However , most of the people (in developed countries , especially in the cities) are not the same people like those in the 70's and 80's. Technology (I mean sophisticated technology) is in our lives for decades now , because it got more accessible and affordable , so people got complacent. So , in my mind  , it'll take some big disturbance (or disturbances) in current socio economic system , in order to people embrace (an mass) those ideas and act upon them. Of course , in undeveloped and some parts of developing countries - it is every day life. There is a question of rules and regulations (which are much more strict and there is more of those , than in the 70's and 80's) , especially in urban areas in developed countries (like UK and EU). Particularly , I think about "kosher" fuels , and permissions to put up some sort of renewable energy device in your back yard , or on the roof ,  or , even , on your window.  Most of the people that live in houses can do something (when it becomes necessity) ,  but , what will people who live in big apartment buildings do (what can they do)?  I have lived through much worse conditions , than like it was in GB in 70's and 80's , so I know first hand , what I'm talking about.

author: @kensmith5694
text:
A few points about saving energy.
A fair chunk of electrical power us used by home computers.  In fact what most people do with their computers could be done by one that consumes under 10W.   If you really want to see how wasteful we are with computers, take a look back at some of the ones that people got hours of entertainment from in the 1970s and 1980s.  Modern electronics could do that with a really tiny power budget.
In a lot of the US, people could get a heat pump installed and save a great pile of money on heating.  This could be even better if the system tended to only heat the room you are in and some infrared lamps were involved.  Infrared warms what it hits and not the air so a room that is perhaps 60F (15C) could be perfectly comfortable.
I think most people have already switched to more efficient light sources.  We still, however, splash light around like it is free.  Back in the days of the oil lamp, you did what needed light near the lamp  Look at streetlights as an example.  You can see a bright sky above a city if you look from a distance at night.  The bats flying around really don't need that light.

author: @Renew_You
text: Robert, you've blessed my day! Thank you! Low tech is cool. In 1998 I created a small company manufacturing stick up sticks to hang paperclips with string attached to hang decorations and educational items from suspended ceiling tiles primarily in classrooms. My target patrons are schoolteachers teaching kindergarten through 3rd grade. A very simple tool that is engineered for cost effective production, esthetic appeal and does the job efficiently. Then I created other teaching aids to increase my market. Generating is generational. Life essentials are simple. The human body needs good air, good water, good salt, good oils, good potassium and exercise... Basically, we can only live without oxygen 3 minutes, without water 3 days. Your milage may vary. Sage advice... keep your liver healthy. Renew You and have fun.

author: @dpuamuishu
text:
I would love to see schemes to add solar and wind generation to schools, they generally have large roof space and they have long periods of the day and year where they should overproduce and supply the grid or store for later.
You could even encourage fossil fuel companies to fund it as a "greenwashing" stunt.
This reduces education costs and eases grid demand.
You could move on to include libraries, NHS buildings, council/government buildings, and so on.
It also strikes me as a good exercise in leading the way and encouraging the public and business sectors.

author: @scotttovey
text:
Reducing energy usage is all fine and good however there is a problem with pursuing that as the only solution as unreasonable environmentalists demand.

The best analogy to explain this dilemma is with daily living expenses.

For the sake of argument, if it requires an individual to spend $50 dollars a day for their basic living, and the individual only earns $25 dollars a day, telling that individual that he must live within his means, is not neither a solution to the problem he is facing, nor is it a realistic. In that hypothetical situation, the individual is being told that he must simply die, rather than being given the tools and skills he needs in order to earn $50 dollars or more a day.

While it can be admirable to seek out way for one to reduce one's energy needs, there is a point when that goal will be detrimental to one's health, and one's life, and one's nation.

This is the same with the delusion that America can reduce it's carbon foot print to such a degree, that it will reverse the so called harm that will be caused by all the additional carbon that China and India are producing.

Anyone that thinks the US is producing that much carbon, needs to participate in that carbon reduction by holding his or her breath until both China and India stop building coal burning electrical generating plants.

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: Carbon in the atmosphere (I mean peak of CO2 in last 50 , or so years) isn't good thing ,  but it is so over hyped and , for sure , by far not the worst thing that we are doing , regarding the pollution of the planet. It is a "catch fraze" to get people to agree to spend more money , they don't have. Even if we (somehow , magically) reduce emissions of CO2 to 1950's level , it still wouldn't  have  any meaningful impact on climate change. US is producing a lots of CO2 and India and China are producing more. But US can repatriate production of goods for it's own use from said countries and than we can calculate the score. Cost of producing the newest I phone in China is around $250 and it's price in US is about $1700 (cheapest ,depending on type of that model). If it was produced in US (somebody calculated it) , because Apple would like to keep their profit margin - it would cost around $3000. India and China has some coal , but not oil and they don't want to be poor anymore , therefore , they produce stuff , so they can earn some money (and they have the buyers lined up, because it is cheaper , than from some other places) . You can build like 20 , or mere coal burning plants for a cost of just one nuclear plant (and there is a potential problem with fuel for that and it would be hard to distribute energy from just one place instead of 20 different places).

replies.author: @scotttovey
@pedjamilosavljevic6235

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: So , why it isn't used more in the industries ? It is not about what is better (for a given problem , or need , at a given place) , but it is all about profit - humans (in the eyes of rulers and companies) have other purposes - like : to consume , work , pay taxes and vote. Even if that would be put to practice , I'll bet that it would be highly regulated and taxed and there would be no benefits for ordinary people . If you are talking about "fighting climate change" , think again. I will believe that humans can do something about that , when I see that someone succeeded in preventing simple flood , never mind to stop the climate change (I am not talking about reducing the pollution of a planet - but even then , price of that should be payed by those who pollute the most ).

author: @mfr58
text: Yes, rather than looking for solutions to maintain business as usual, we would do well to reassess our needs. Many take this to be a call for austerity, a step backwards. However if we take a more wholistic view of our needs in the context of nature around us, our creativity will emerge in more healthy and efficient ways of being in the world.
